devanagari: चीरहरणलीला । श्रीशुक उवाच । हेमन्ते प्रथमे मासि नन्दव्रजकुमारिकाः । चेरुर्हविष्यं भुञ्जानाः कात्यायन्यर्चनव्रतम् ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: cīraharaṇalīlā śrīśuka uvāca hemante prathame māsi nandavrajakumārikāḥ cerurhaviṣyaṃ bhuñjānāḥ kātyāyanyarcanavratam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
In the first month (Mārgaśīrṣa), in the season of Hemanta,[1] maids in the Vraja (cowherd-settlement of Nanda), observed the vow of adoring goddess Kātyāyanī, eating only pure food fit for being offered to sacrificial fire.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
This season consists of the following months of the Hindu calendar—Mārgaśīrṣa and Pauṣa.

devanagari: आप्लुत्याम्भसि कालिन्द्या जलान्ते चोदितेऽरुणे । कृत्वा प्रतिकृतिं देवीं आनर्चुः नृप सैकतीम् ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: āplutyāmbhasi kālindyā jalānte codite kṛtvā pratikṛtiṃ devīṃ ānarcuḥ nṛpa saikatīm

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ cud ], [vocative dual from √ cud ], [accusative dual from √ cud ], [locative single from √ cud ]
[nominative dual from √ cud ], [vocative single from √ cud ], [vocative dual from √ cud ], [accusative dual from √ cud ]

translation: Having taken bath in the waters of the Yamunā at day-break, they made an image in sand of the goddess, just near the river water, on the bank, and worshipped her with sandal-paste, fragrant flowers, offering her propitiatory oblations, burning frank-incense, waving lights before her, and presenting other miscellaneous gifts of varying costs, along with tender shoots, fruits and unbroken grains of rice.

devanagari: गन्धैर्माल्यैः सुरभिभिः बलिभिर्धूपदीपकैः । उच्चावचैश्चोपहारैः प्रवालफल तण्डुलैः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: gandhairmālyaiḥ surabhibhiḥ balibhirdhūpadīpakaiḥ uccāvacaiścopahāraiḥ pravālaphala taṇḍulaiḥ
translation: Having taken bath in the waters of the Yamunā at day-break, they made an image in sand of the goddess, just near the river water, on the bank, and worshipped her with sandal-paste, fragrant flowers, offering her propitiatory oblations, burning frank-incense, waving lights before her, and presenting other miscellaneous gifts of varying costs, along with tender shoots, fruits and unbroken grains of rice.

devanagari: कात्यायनि महामाये महायोगिन्यधीश्वरि । नन्दगोपसुतं देवि पतिं मे कुरु ते नमः । इति मन्त्रं जपन्त्यस्ताः पूजां चक्रुः कुमारिकाः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: kātyāyani mahāmāye mahāyoginyadhīśvari nandagopasutaṃ devi patiṃ me kuru te namaḥ iti mantraṃ japantyastāḥ pūjāṃ cakruḥ kumārikāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ su class 5 verb], [accusative single from √ su class 5 verb]
[nominative single from √ sū class 6 verb], [accusative single from √ sū class 6 verb]

[nominative plural from √ jap class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ jap class 1 verb]

translation: Those virgins performed the worship of the goddess, each repeating the prayer (as a mantra or incantation): “Oh goddess Kātyāyanī! Oh great deluding potency of the Lord! Oh great yoginī (of great Super-human powers)! Oh absolute ruler of the universe! Be pleased to grant the son of cowherd Nanda (i.e. Kṛṣṇa) as my husband![2]
purport:
Footnote [2]:
This Sanskrit verse is regarded as a mantra and it is to be repeated 1,600 times a day, during the period of Kātyāyanī-vrata—KD.22.62.
VT and Sārārthadarśinī quote Nārada-pañcarātra to show that every mantra about Kṛṣṇa has goddess Durgā as the presiding deity. Hence the justification of this vrata and japa for getting Kṛṣṇa as a husband.

devanagari: एवं मासं व्रतं चेरुः कुमार्यः कृष्णचेतसः । भद्रकालीं समानर्चुः भूयान्नन्दसुतः पतिः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ māsaṃ vrataṃ ceruḥ kumāryaḥ kṛṣṇacetasaḥ bhadrakālīṃ samānarcuḥ bhūyānnandasutaḥ patiḥ
translation: The maidens who have set their heart on Kṛṣṇa, observed the vow for the period of a month, worshipping goddess Bhadrakālī with the prayer, “May Nanda’s son be our husband”.

devanagari: ऊषस्युत्थाय गोत्रैः स्वैरन्योन्या बद्धबाहवः । कृष्णं उच्चैर्जगुर्यान्त्यः कालिन्द्यां स्नातुमन्वहम् ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: ūṣasyutthāya gotraiḥ svairanyonyā baddhabāhavaḥ kṛṣṇaṃ uccairjaguryāntyaḥ kālindyāṃ snātumanvaham
translation: Getting up early in the morning, and calling others by their names (to wake them up), they sang in loud chorus the glory of Lord Kṛṣṇa, as they went to bathe in the Yamunā clasping each other’s hand.

devanagari: नद्याः कदाचिदागत्य तीरे निक्षिप्य पूर्ववत् । वासांसि कृष्णं गायन्त्यो विजह्रुः सलिले मुदा ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: nadyāḥ kadācidāgatya tīre nikṣipya pūrvavat vāsāṃsi kṛṣṇaṃ gāyantyo vijahruḥ salile mudā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ nad ], [vocative plural from √ nad ]
[nominative plural from √ nad ], [vocative plural from √ nad ], [accusative plural from √ nad ]

translation: On one occasion (on the last day of that vow which was a full-moon day), they, as usual, left their garments on the bank, and singing the glory of Kṛṣṇa, they sported in the water merrily (as that was the concluding day of their vow). Thus they concentrated on him in mind, word and deed).[3]
purport:
Footnote [3]:
evaṃ mānasa-vācika-kāyikaikatānatvaṃ darśitam—VT.
The poet-annotator KD. says that the maidens imagined the bluish water of Yamunā as the person of Kṛṣṇa and embraced Him (in the form of water) and kissed it etc.—KD. 22.84-87.

devanagari: भगवान् तदभिप्रेत्य कृष्णो योगेश्वरेश्वरः । वयस्यैरावृतस्तत्र गतस्तत्कर्मसिद्धये ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: bhagavān tadabhipretya kṛṣṇo yogeśvareśvaraḥ vayasyairāvṛtastatra gatastatkarmasiddhaye
translation: Understanding their intention, the glorious Lord Kṛṣṇa, the Supreme Master of all other experts in yoga,[4] surrounded by his companions,[5] went there to grant them the fruit of their vow.[6]
purport:
Footnote [4]:
The epithets bhagavān yogeśvareśvaraḥ shows that it is the ‘eternal metaphysical Lord—the third aspect of the Reality’ which is implied here and not the human Vāsudeva Kṛṣṇa (viḍe Intro. XLVIII-XLIX Bhāgavata Purāṇa v.l. I). KD. underscores the same, “He became many to satisfy them.”—KD. 22.99.

Footnote [5]:
VT. quotes Gautamī Tantra and shows that these companions are ‘Intellect, ego, mind, heart’.

Footnote [6]:
Subodhinī thinks that the maidens have committed the following faults: nude bath, breaking of silence and playing while observing a vow. To purge them of these, Lord Kṛṣṇa came there and by his look did it.

devanagari: तासां वासांस्युपादाय नीपमारुह्य सत्वरः । हसद्भिः प्रहसन् बालैः परिहासमुवाच ह ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: tāsāṃ vāsāṃsyupādāya nīpamāruhya satvaraḥ hasadbhiḥ prahasan bālaiḥ parihāsamuvāca ha

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: It is traditionally reported that he collected all their garments and quickly climbed a Kadamba tree (nearby). Laughing loudly, he spoke to them in fun, while (other) boys also kept a-laughing.

devanagari: अत्रागत्याबलाः कामं स्वं स्वं वासः प्रगृह्यताम् । सत्यं ब्रवाणि नो नर्म यद्यूयं व्रतकर्शिताः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: atrāgatyābalāḥ kāmaṃ svaṃ svaṃ vāsaḥ pragṛhyatām satyaṃ bravāṇi no narma yadyūyaṃ vratakarśitāḥ

translation: “Oh delicate girls! As you are exhausted by observing a religious vow, I am not joking. Earnestly I tell you. Let each of you come here at will, and receive her respective garments.

devanagari: न मयोदितपूर्वं वा अनृतं तदिमे विदुः । एकैकशः प्रतीच्छध्वं सहैवेति सुमध्यमाः ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: na mayoditapūrvaṃ vā anṛtaṃ tadime viduḥ ekaikaśaḥ pratīcchadhvaṃ sahaiveti sumadhyamāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active second single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: As these (cowherd) boys know it, I have never spoken a lie up till now. Oh girls with beautiful waist (Oh girls of pure hearts)! You may come here one by one or all together, and take your own clothes.[7]
purport:
Footnote [7]:
It is the erotically tinged devotion of Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavas which makes VT. state that Kṛṣṇa was desirous of seeing the central part of their bodies,—
yuṣmākam madhyabhāgādisaundaryam eva draṣṭum iṣyate,
na ca vastrair etair matprayojanam iti nigūḍho bhāvaḥ /
It is on the background of Tantricism that this Vaiṣṇavism was developed. Hence the commentator’s words should not be superficially understood at their face value in view of note on page 1396. Subodhinī says Kṛṣṇa wanted to see their hearts, interpreting madhya as ‘heart’. Other annotators have just passed it over. As Kṛṣṇa’s physical age was only six years and four months at this time, the girls wishing to marry him must have been presumably less than six years of age and as such this episode appears allegorical.

devanagari: तस्य तत् क्ष्वेलितं दृष्ट्वा गोप्यः प्रेमपरिप्लुताः । व्रीडिताः प्रेक्ष्य चान्योन्यं जातहासा न निर्ययुः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: tasya tat kṣvelitaṃ dṛṣṭvā gopyaḥ premapariplutāḥ vrīḍitāḥ prekṣya cānyonyaṃ jātahāsā na niryayuḥ

translation: Perceiving (the implications of) his joke, the cowherd girls were overcome with love. Bashfully they looked at each other and smiled, but none came out of the water.

devanagari: एवं ब्रुवति गोविन्दे नर्मणाऽक्षिप्तचेतसः । आकण्ठमग्नाः शीतोदे वेपमानास्तमब्रुवन् ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ bruvati govinde narmaṇā ākaṇṭhamagnāḥ śītode vepamānāstamabruvan

translation: When Kṛṣṇa was speaking to them jocularly, the girls got confused in their mind. Keeping themselves immersed in the cold water upto the neck, they requested him, while shivering with cold.

devanagari: मानयं भोः कृथास्त्वां तु नन्दगोपसुतं प्रियम् । जानीमोऽङ्ग व्रजश्लाघ्यं देहि वासांसि वेपिताः ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: mānayaṃ bhoḥ kṛthāstvāṃ tu nandagopasutaṃ priyam jānīmo

translation: “Oh dear Kṛṣṇa! Pray, do not be unjust to us. Darling! We know that you, the son of Nanda, are our beloved, whom the whole of Vraja praises. Pray, do return our clothes to us, as we are shivering (with biting cold).

devanagari: श्यामसुन्दर ते दास्यः करवाम तवोदितम् । देहि वासांसि धर्मज्ञ नो चेद् राज्ञे ब्रुवाम हे ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: śyāmasundara te dāsyaḥ karavāma tavoditam dehi vāsāṃsi dharmajña no ced rājñe bruvāma he

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ vad class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ vad class 1 verb]

translation: Oh beautiful Kṛṣṇa of charmingly bluish complexion! We are your servants. We all shall carry out your behests. You are a knower of dharma[8] (righteous conduct). Fray return our clothes, else we shall (be constrained to) report the matter to the king (Nanda)” (threatened the elderly gopī’s).
purport:
Footnote [8]:
Subodhinī quotes a smṛti text which prohibits the seeing of the secret parts of a girl, sexual act of beasts, a naked woman or one whose breasts are exposed to view:
kanyā-yoniṃ paśu-krīḍām nagna-strīṃ prakaṭa-stanīṃ nāvalokayet /
Hence as a knower of dharma, Kṛṣṇa should return the clothes.

devanagari: श्रीभगवानुवाच । भवत्यो यदि मे दास्यो मयोक्तं वा करिष्यथ । अत्रागत्य स्ववासांसि प्रतीच्छन्तु शुचिस्मिताः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: śrībhagavānuvāca bhavatyo yadi me dāsyo mayoktaṃ vā kariṣyatha atrāgatya svavāsāṃsi pratīcchantu śucismitāḥ

translation:
The glorious Lord said:
“If you are my servants (as you say), and undertake to abide by my biddings, come here and take back each your (respective) clothes, have a countenance beaming with a smile.
16(A). Padaratnāvalī and VT. record a half-verse
Otherwise, I will not return the garments. What will the angry chief do to me?”.

devanagari: ततो जलाशयात् सर्वा दारिकाः शीतवेपिताः । पाणिभ्यां योनिमाच्छाद्य प्रोत्तेरुः शीतकर्शिताः ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: tato jalāśayāt sarvā dārikāḥ śītavepitāḥ pāṇibhyāṃ yonimācchādya protteruḥ śītakarśitāḥ

translation: At this, all the girls shivering with cold and covering their private parts with the palms of their hands, came out of the water, frozen (lit. shrunk) with cold.

devanagari: भगवानाहता वीक्ष्य शुद्ध भावप्रसादितः । स्कन्धे निधाय वासांसि प्रीतः प्रोवाच सस्मितम् ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: bhagavānāhatā vīkṣya śuddha bhāvaprasāditaḥ skandhe nidhāya vāsāṃsi prītaḥ provāca sasmitam

translation: Perceiving their virginity,[9] the glorious Lord who was propitiated with the purity of their hearts, placed their clothes on the branch of the tree and smilingly addressed them, with great pleasure.

devanagari: यूयं विवस्त्रा यदपो धृतव्रता । व्यगाहतैतत् तदु देवहेलनम् । बद्ध्वाञ्जलिं मूर्ध्न्यपनुत्तयेंऽहसः । कृत्वा नमोऽधो वसनं प्रगृह्यताम् ॥ १९...
english_devnagari: yūyaṃ vivastrā yadapo dhṛtavratā vyagāhataitat tadu devahelanam baddhvāñjaliṃ mūrdhnyapanuttayeṃ kṛtvā namo

[nominative single from √ yu class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ yu class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ yu class 6 verb], [accusative single from √ yu class 6 verb], [nominative single from √ yu class 9 verb], [accusative single from √ yu class 9 verb]
[nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation:
“It is a positive offence against gods, that you entered water in a nude state,[10] after undertaking the observance of a religious vow. For the expiation of this sin, you bow raising up your folded palms to your heads, and lay prostrate (on the ground) after which you accept your (respective) clothings.”
[Additional verses in the Texts of Bhāgavata Candrikā and Padaratnāvalī.]
20(A). Hearing this command full of religious significance, from the great Soul (Lord Kṛṣṇa), the damsels paid obeisance to him with one hand, covering their private part with the other.
21(B). Seeing them (violate the proper way of bowing to the Lord), the glorious Lord uttered this observation full of righteousness (and concerning religious formality), “Those who are experts in Vedas know it (and have laid it down) that he who bows to god Acyuta (Kṛṣṇa or Viṣṇu) with one hand only, should be punished by cutting off the other hand (not used in bowing).
22(C). Hence, a person should pay respects to his Lord by both of his hands. You, therefore, comply that way, and that will please me the better”.
purport:
Footnote [10]:
Cf. na nagnaḥ karhicit snāyāt /—Mahābhārata Anu. 104.51

devanagari: इत्यच्युतेनाभिहितं व्रजाबला । मत्वा विवस्त्राप्लवनं व्रतच्युतिम् । तत्पूर्तिकामास्तदशेषकर्मणां । साक्षात्कृतं नेमुरवद्यमृग् यतः ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: ityacyutenābhihitaṃ vrajābalā matvā vivastrāplavanaṃ vratacyutim tatpūrtikāmāstadaśeṣakarmaṇāṃ sākṣātkṛtaṃ nemuravadyamṛg yataḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative plural from √ i class 2 verb], [ablative single from √ i class 2 verb], [genitive single from √ i class 2 verb]
[ablative single from √ i class 2 verb], [genitive single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: Considering what was explained to them by Lord Kṛṣṇa (Acyuta), the maids of Vraja realized that taking a plunge (bath) in a nude state is a transgression in the observation of their vow. Being desirous of completely fulfilling the vow, with all the related rites and formalities, they bowed to Lord Kṛṣṇa, the very embodiment of the fruit and religious rites, directly present before them. And it was he who washes off all sins, flaws and transgressions.

devanagari: तास्तथावनता दृष्ट्वा भगवान् देवकीसुतः । वासांसि ताभ्यः प्रायच्छत् करुणस्तेन तोषितः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: tāstathāvanatā dṛṣṭvā bhagavān devakīsutaḥ vāsāṃsi tābhyaḥ prāyacchat karuṇastena toṣitaḥ
translation: Seeing them bowing down humbly before him, the merciful Lord Kṛṣṇa, the son of Devakī, was pleased with them, and returned to them all their clothes.

devanagari: दृढं प्रलब्धास्त्रपया च हापिताः । प्रस्तोभिताः क्रीडनवच्च कारिताः । वस्त्राणि चैवापहृतान्यथाप्यमुं । ता नाभ्यसूयन् प्रियसङ्गनिर्वृताः ...
english_devnagari: dṛḍhaṃ pralabdhāstrapayā ca hāpitāḥ prastobhitāḥ krīḍanavacca kāritāḥ vastrāṇi caivāpahṛtānyathāpyamuṃ tā nābhyasūyan priyasaṅganirvṛtāḥ

translation: Though they were badly deceived by the criticism of their nudity (in verse 19), were compelled to give up bashfulness, were ridiculed (by saying that he was not joking (verse 10), were treated like puppets and robbed of their- clothings, they were so much satisfied with the company of their beloved that they bore no displeasure towards him.

devanagari: परिधाय स्ववासांसि प्रेष्ठसङ्गमसज्जिताः । गृहीतचित्ता नो चेलुः तस्मिन् लज्जायितेक्षणाः ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: paridhāya svavāsāṃsi preṣṭhasaṅgamasajjitāḥ gṛhītacittā no celuḥ tasmin lajjāyitekṣaṇāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ sajj class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ sajj class 1 verb]

[nominative plural from √ sañj ], [vocative plural from √ sañj ]
[nominative plural from √ sañj ], [vocative plural from √ sañj ], [accusative plural from √ sañj ]

translation: They put on their clothes. So charmed were they with the company of the Lord, and their hearts were so much attracted, that they did not stir away from that place but continued to cast their bashful glances at him.

devanagari: तासां विज्ञाय भगवान् स्वपादस्पर्शकाम्यया । धृतव्रतानां सङ्कल्पं आह दामोदरोऽबलाः ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: tāsāṃ vijñāya bhagavān svapādasparśakāmyayā dhṛtavratānāṃ saṅkalpaṃ āha dāmodaro
translation: Understanding that their motive of undertaking the observance (of Kātyāyanī) vow was the longing to touch his feet, the glorious Lord Dāmodara (Kṛṣṇa) addressed the maidens:

devanagari: सङ्कल्पो विदितः साध्व्यो भवतीनां मदर्चनम् । मयानुमोदितः सोऽसौ सत्यो भवितुमर्हति ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: saṅkalpo viditaḥ sādhvyo bhavatīnāṃ madarcanam mayānumoditaḥ so
translation: “Oh chaste ladies! Your object (in observing the Kātyāyanī vow) viz. to worship me, is already known to me. It is approved of by me, and it shall be realized.

devanagari: न मय्यावेशितधियां कामः कामाय कल्पते । भर्जिता क्वथिता धाना प्रायो बीजाय नेशते ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: na mayyāveśitadhiyāṃ kāmaḥ kāmāya kalpate bharjitā kvathitā dhānā prāyo bījāya neśate

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ kvath class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ kvath class 1 verb]
[nominative plural from √ kvath class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ kvath class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ kvath class 1 verb]

translation: Your desire for my company is not censurable, for your longing to have enjoyment with me will not again lead to a birth in Saṃsāra. A seed once fried or boiled does not sprout.

devanagari: याताबला व्रजं सिद्धा मयेमा रंस्यथा क्षपाः । यदुद्दिश्य व्रतमिदं चेरुरार्यार्चनं सतीः ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: yātābalā vrajaṃ siddhā mayemā raṃsyathā kṣapāḥ yaduddiśya vratamidaṃ cerurāryārcanaṃ satīḥ

translation: Return to Vraja, Oh girls, as your object is accomplished. You will spend the autumnal nights in enjoyment with me—the object for which you, good ladies, have observed the vow of the worship of Kātyāyanī.”

devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच । इत्यादिष्टा भगवता लब्धकामाः कुमारिकाः । ध्यायन्त्यस्तत् पदाम्भोजं कृच्छ्रात् निर्विविशुर्व्रजम् ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvāca ityādiṣṭā bhagavatā labdhakāmāḥ kumārikāḥ dhyāyantyastat padāmbhojaṃ kṛcchrāt nirviviśurvrajam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
Thus commanded by the Lord, the maidens whose object was accomplished, continued to contemplate in their minds the lotus-feet of the Lord, and returned to Vraja most grudgingly (lit. with great difficulty).

devanagari: अथ गोपैः परिवृतो भगवान् देवकीसुतः । वृन्दावनाद् गतो दूरं चारयन् गाः सहाग्रजः ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: atha gopaiḥ parivṛto bhagavān devakīsutaḥ vṛndāvanād gato dūraṃ cārayan gāḥ sahāgrajaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ car ], [vocative single from √ car ]

translation: The glorious Lorḍ, the son of Devakī, accompanied by Balarāma and surrounded by cowherds, went to a great distance from Vṛndāvana, grazing the cattle.

devanagari: निदघार्कातपे तिग्मे छायाभिः स्वाभिरात्मनः । आतपत्रायितान् वीक्ष्य द्रुमानाह व्रजौकसः ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: nidaghārkātape tigme chāyābhiḥ svābhirātmanaḥ ātapatrāyitān vīkṣya drumānāha vrajaukasaḥ
translation: Perceiving that the trees served as umbrellas by spreading their shade in the scorching heat of the summer sun, Kṛṣṇa addressed to his cowherd friends, the residents of Vraja:

devanagari: हे स्तोककृष्ण हे अंशो श्रीदामन् सुबलार्जुन । विशालर्षभ तेजस्विन् देवप्रस्थ वरूथप ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: he stokakṛṣṇa he aṃśo śrīdāman subalārjuna viśālarṣabha tejasvin devaprastha varūthapa
translation: “Oh little Kṛṣṇa, Oh Aṃśu, Oh Śrīdāman, Subala, Arjuna, Viśāla, Ṛṣabha, Tejasvin, Devaprastha and Varūthapa!

devanagari: पश्यतैतान् महाभागान् परार्थैकान्तजीवितान् । वातवर्षातपहिमान् सहन्तो वारयन्ति नः ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: paśyataitān mahābhāgān parārthaikāntajīvitān vātavarṣātapahimān sahanto vārayanti naḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ sah class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ sah class 1 verb]
[nominative plural from √ vṛ ], [vocative plural from √ vṛ ], [accusative plural from √ vṛ ]

translation: Have a look at these great blessed souls who live only for the welfare of others, and themselves undergo (for others) the severity of stormy winds, heavy showers, summer heat and snow, and save them off from us.

devanagari: अहो एषां वरं जन्म सर्व प्राण्युपजीवनम् । सुजनस्येव येषां वै विमुखा यान्ति नार्थिनः ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: aho eṣāṃ varaṃ janma sarva prāṇyupajīvanam sujanasyeva yeṣāṃ vai vimukhā yānti nārthinaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual], [nominative plural], [vocative dual], [vocative plural], [accusative dual], [accusative plural], [locative single]
[present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: The birth of the trees is verily the most blessed in the world, as it contributes to the well-being of all creatures. Just as no needy person returns disappointed from benevolent persons, so is the case with a person who approaches these for shelter.

devanagari: पत्रपुष्पफलच्छाया मूलवल्कलदारुभिः । गन्धनिर्यासभस्मास्थि तोक्मैः कामान् वितन्वते ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: patrapuṣpaphalacchāyā mūlavalkaladārubhiḥ gandhaniryāsabhasmāsthi tokmaiḥ kāmān vitanvate
translation: They fulfil the desires (needs) of others by means of their leaves, flowers, fruits, shade, roots, bark, wood, fragrance, gums or exudations, ashes, coals and tender leaves.

devanagari: एतावत् जन्मसाफल्यं देहिनामिह देहिषु । प्राणैरर्थैर्धिया वाचा श्रेय एवाचरेत् सदा ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: etāvat janmasāphalyaṃ dehināmiha dehiṣu prāṇairarthairdhiyā vācā śreya evācaret sadā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ śrā class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ śrā class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ śrā class 4 verb]
[nominative single from √ śrai class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ śrai class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ śrai class 4 verb]

translation: That a person should sacrifice his life, wealth, intellect and speech for doing what is beneficial to others, is the farthest limit of the usefulness of embodied beings for their fellow creatures.”

devanagari: इति प्रवालस्तबक फलपुष्पदलोत्करैः । तरूणां नम्रशाखानां मध्यतो यमुनां गतः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: iti pravālastabaka phalapuṣpadalotkaraiḥ tarūṇāṃ namraśākhānāṃ madhyato yamunāṃ gataḥ
translation: Praising them (the trees) thus, the Lord proceeded to the Yamunā passing through the avenue of trees whose branches were bent low with clusters of young leaves, foliage, bunches of fruits and flowers and other leaves.

devanagari: तत्र गाः पाययित्वापः सुमृष्टाः शीतलाः शिवाः । ततो नृप स्वयं गोपाः कामं स्वादु पपुर्जलम् ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: tatra gāḥ pāyayitvāpaḥ sumṛṣṭāḥ śītalāḥ śivāḥ tato nṛpa svayaṃ gopāḥ kāmaṃ svādu papurjalam
translation: Having made their cattle drink that very sweet, cool and healthy water of the Yamunā (to their fill), the cowherds themselves drank that sweet water to their heart’s content, Oh king.

devanagari: तस्या उपवने कामं चारयन्तः पशून् नृप । कृष्णरामौ उवुपागम्य क्षुधार्ता इदमब्रवन् ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: tasyā upavane kāmaṃ cārayantaḥ paśūn nṛpa kṛṣṇarāmau uvupāgamya kṣudhārtā idamabravan

[nominative single from √ ru class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ ru class 1 verb]

translation:
The cowherd pastured their cattle in the groves on the bank of the Yamunā. Being oppressed with hunger they approached Balarāma and Kṛṣṇa and addressed as follows:
